chunk_summary: This section outlines tiered nutrition investigations for chronic cardiorespiratory disease, emphasizing decision-making based on test availability and cost, with 'if unavailable' branches to avoid delays in care.
learning_objectives: Understand the tiered approach to nutrition investigations in chronic cardiorespiratory disease.
Identify key tests in Tier 1, Tier 2, and Tier 3 and their clinical implications.
Apply 'if unavailable' strategies to manage resource-limited settings effectively.
Recognize the importance of avoiding delays in calorie escalation and feeding safety.
key_facts: Tier 1 tests include electrolytes (sodium, potassium, bicarbonate), creatinine, glucose, and hemoglobin, critical for feeding tolerance and calorie escalation safety.
Tier 2 tests are mechanism-targeted, such as CRP for inflammation, fecal elastase for CF-related malabsorption, and VFSS/FEES for aspiration risk.
If fecal elastase is unavailable, treat classic steatorrhea and failure to gain weight as pancreatic insufficiency and dose PERT accordingly.
If VFSS/FEES is unavailable, manage feed-linked desaturation or choking as aspiration risk and adjust feeding routes to prevent pneumonia.
Tier 3 tests, including phosphate and magnesium, are essential for detecting refeeding syndrome in severe malnutrition or prolonged inadequate intake.
reference_quote: When fecal elastase is unavailable, treat classic steatorrhea and failure to gain as pancreatic insufficiency until proven otherwise and dose PERT appropriately. (Source: https://www.cff.org/medical-professionals/pancreatic-enzymes-clinical-care-guidelines...)
document_key_topics: Feeding safety in chronic cardiorespiratory disease
Risk stratification for malnutrition and refeeding syndrome
